speaker: interviewer
text: It is Friday, June 8th. And we're talking today in Springfield, New Jersey, with survivor Norman Salsitz. And we are talking now about what happened when you were liberated and the war is over. And where did you go after liberation? Did you return to your town? Did you go to another place? I want to know how you got to America, which is where you went eventually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now you said the war was over. The war wasn't over. Because this was-- this was August the 1st, 1944. We still had a long time till the war-- till the war ended.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was liberated. I didn't go back to my town and I never went back to my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Never?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Till today, I never went back because I knew what's waiting for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go a that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went--after I went to Rzeszow, right away, I joined the Polish army. I wanted-- I joined the Polish army. I was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: With which name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Name? I-- I got a new name-- in the [NON-ENGLISH], my name was Tadeusz Yadech. This was my false-- not false, my birth-- baptismal paper. And my name became Tadeusz Zaleski.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you picked a third name?

speaker: interviewee
text: A third name. And I joined the army as Tadoush Zaleski. I was sent to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Pole, not Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: As Pole, no Jew. I was sent to Lublin. Lublin was, in this time, the temporary capital in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you join the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted-- if I survived the war and there was a war going on in that I can take care fighting against the Germans, I had to join the army. Because we had to fight against the Germans. There was still a war. And I was sent to Lublin, and I was assigned to a-- I was sent to a officers' military school. And when I went through this military school in-- in Lublin, meanwhile, I legalize my group in the woods. So if I legalize that there was an official group, and especially I had proof that we attacked an SS in the substation-- which this sergeant, from the [NON-ENGLISH] was killed, once I legalized it, I got automatically a rank as a captain. And when I finished this military school, exactly the time when the last offensive towards Berlin, January the 22nd, started. And I was assigned to a special group. And I was assigned that we, our group, will be the one who will liberate Krakow. And Krakow was one of-- not one, it was the most important town because the governor Frank was in Krakow. And it was the most historical town.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our group had to liberate. And we went out towards Krakow. And how we came to Krakow, it's a long story. Finally, we came to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Krakow two days before Krakow was liberated. Officially, Krakow was liberated on 19th of January, '45. I was already in Krakow the 17th of January, 1945. Because Krakow, we knew, had to be destroyed completely. The Germans made a few hundred mines under important houses. And, also, they build columns, high columns, on each cross street, which was most important. This had to be detonated with one push, with one button. And we were sent, three of us were sent to find out. We-- we were assigned to the 59th army of the First Ukrainian Army, of the Russian army. And we had-- were sent out to find out if we somehow will know who can tell us about this destruction. And through channels and channels, I came to the house, Eulucia-- Street Eulucia Leia 19. And this was the office of the company Myer, Reider, and Krauss, who built those columns and the mines. And we came in and we met there-- I've met there a German girl. Before we came in, we-- and we wanted to get the plans-- before we came in, we decided, as soon as we get the plans, the person who will be-- will have the plan has to be killed, not to let know the Germans that we have the plans. And we came in and there was the most beautiful girl I ever saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because she was so beautiful, I hated her more, because I saw my five sisters, I saw my girlfriend that they are dead. And why should this German bitch-- this how I called her, be alive-- have beautiful furniture, chandeliers, light? And it was cold outside. This was the 19th of-- the 18th of January.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in and we started to interrogate her. We wanted to have-- I wanted to have the plans. She didn't admit that she's German. We knew that a German girl was left. She didn't admit. She said she's Polish and her name is Felicia Merashevska. She showed me her IDs. I didn't believe her. And I started to question her, question her. Finally, she said to one of the officers who was with me, he was a native of Krakow, and he had a very Jewish face. She said she wants to talk to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: She went in in the second room, she spoke to him. And she said to him what do you want from me. I'm the same as you. Said, I'm a Polish officer, you are a German. She said, no, you are a Jew and I'm a Jew. How do you know? I recognized. He ask-- she asked him from where are you, and from where are you. She said I'm from Stanislaw. From where are you? From Krakow. She said from Krakow, I had a schoolmate in the Hebrew gymnasium. His name was Oscar Margolis. This officer became white. He said Oscar Margolis is my cousin. Is he alive? And if he knew that he went to a Hebrew gymnasium, so this girl must be Jewish. He came out and he told me this, and I said you must be crazy. Did you know he was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I knew. He knew I was Jewish. And he said to me you must be-- must be crazy. She said she'll prove it to me. So even-- I made the joke. I said, yes, she proved it. She showed you that she is circumcised. So I-- he laughed. And then we started to question her more. Once I knew already, so I started to ask her what language do you speak. Because I didn't want to speak in Polish, the other people should know. Because it was very dangerous. The Germans were still in Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I speak Russian, English, German, Hebrew. So when she said Hebrew, I said wait a second. Speak to me Hebrew. She said to me how would I speak to you Hebrew when your face shows that you are the biggest anti-Semite? With you, or how will you understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, never mind, speak. And she started to speak to me in Hebrew. And she said to me you are a big dope, that you will give me such a hard time. When I saw that she spoke Hebrew, so I still didn't believe her. I ask her when do Jews pray Kol Nidre. So she didn't think about it. She said Kol Nidre Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the minute she said Yom Kippur, she stopped and she started to have tears in her eyes. And her eyes opened and she said, how-- how do you know-- and she started to cry. How do you know about Kol Nidre? How do you want-- and I cried too and I cry now. She said how do you know about Kol Nidre. You want to tell me you too-- you too? And I said, yes, I too. And then I knew she's Jewish. And then I ask her for the plans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she gave me the plans. And with the plans, I run back to our headquarters. And this is how Krakow was saved. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how you met your future wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how I met her. I didn't-- right away, it was not right away. But, after a while, we started to go out. And this is how I met my future wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you both come to America together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- I run away. I run away from Poland because we decided to go to Palestine in this time.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two of us. Because I said if I survive the war, and I have experience from the underground, from the Polish army, Palestine, the Jews don't have an army. I have to go there and I will be important there. They need me. And we run away to Germany. In Germany, I started to work in the Brihah. The Brihah is a group who used to bring over Jews from Poland to Berlin, from Berlin to Munich, from Munich to Italy. I was the one who went twice a week to Germany, to Berlin, and brought over groups to Munich. In Munich, somebody else took over. When I escaped, my wife remained and she was arrested. She was arrested by the secret police to find out where I am. Finally, she denied that we are married because we married on our real name. And my name was Tadoush Zaleski and her name was Felicia Merashevska. They let her out. And then, later, she came to Munich. And in Munich, we registered to Palestine, but we had to go illegal. And she didn't want to go to be put in in Cyprus in the other camp. So we decided and we said we will register to America and to Palestine. Which will come first, there we will go. And from America, we can always go to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- America came first. So we came to America. And we decided, from here, to go to Palestine. The first money we made in 1949, we went to Palestine. We wanted to settle there. We didn't have no children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had her sister, a grandmother, three uncles, the whole family from Germany. And they said to her go back to America. We are very bad off in Palestine. You go to America, try to make money, and send us money. My idealism was gone because it was already after the war. So I-- what I wanted to go into fight for Palestine, because if somebody will tell me during the German occupation that I will be able to fight for Palestine, I would say I want to be in Palestine one day and to have a rifle in my hand, and to fight for Palestine one day, and then I should be shot. And this will be enough for me. Until today, if somebody would ask me what my biggest mistake was, I always say that I didn't go to Palestine. Not that America was bad for me, America was better than I ever imagined.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you still regret not going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I regret, why? Because I survived the war. And my place was to be there and to fight for Palestine. And if I-- it means for Israel-- and if I didn't go, I feel bad today. And because, America, like I said, I have no regrets. I mean, I have no complaints. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand. What did you do when you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to America, the first year, I had a lot of jobs. Not important jobs, I didn't have a trade. Now I was trained in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to America, I was 27 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in January 1947. So I didn't-- I started to have jobs. Most of the time, they fired me. I have the big mouth. In most-- sometimes, I quit. Finally, I started my own business. I became-- I had an installment business. Installment business, I sold from house to house. And it was a wonderful business.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. In the beginning, the first day, when I came to America and I worked in the factories, I made $21 a week. When I started to sell from house to house, I took hundred dozen of socks, and I sold them in one day. And I made $1 profit on a dozen. I made, the first day, $100 in a day. And, here, I worked for $21 a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you say you sold them on installments. So you sold a pair of socks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a pair of socks, no. This was too small items. But, then, later, from socks, I went down to curtains. I wind up with furniture. Mostly, I sold to the lady. I find her furniture, appliances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you bought them and then you sold them to people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a warehouse and I bought them. And I sold them to people on installment. And my route was in Jersey City, that majority were Polish people. And I spoke Polish. So this was-- and I was in installment business almost-- almost 15 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a wonderful business, was very good. Then, later, I started to build. And I became a builder. I had a customer, he was a Italian man, a builder, but he didn't have the guts to build houses. He made repairs.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out and I bought the first lot. I said let's be partners. Then I bought two, then I bought five, then I bought land, and I developed that land. And, this, I was a builder from '65 till '87. In '87, I retired. And now what about your family? You had children.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, I had one daughter. We wanted to have-- in the beginning, we didn't want to have children. We thought that we don't want to bring in a child into a world like this. But, later, we realized we wanted to have, but we couldn't have children. It took 11 years till our daughter was born. When she was born, naturally, our life was complete. And-- and then, I bought a house. And she was a year old, we moved to Springfield. And we live here since then. Every year, practically, we go to Israel. I was in Israel already about 38 times, and I started to be involved in all kind of Jewish organizations, and special in Holocaust organizations. And this is how life goes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and do you have-- your daughter married?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter is married. She's a lawyer. She has her own office in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband is a medical doctor. He's an oncologist hematologist. He's in that department from-- from Harvard Medical School. And now he's the head in Beth Israel from Harvard Medical School for AIDS, people with AIDS who have cancer. He goes around the all over the world lecturing. And I have three grandchildren. The oldest is 12 years old. His name is Dustin. The second is seven years old. His name is Arn. And the third is Michael, he's five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they as feisty as you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- everybody has a different characteristic. Like for the oldest one, they all go to Solomon Schechter. Because I said to my daughter, if they go to Solomon Schechter, I pay for it. Not long ago, a few weeks ago, she sent me a bill for $27,000 for three boys for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: The oldest likes money. And the oldest said-- what does he ask my daughter? Who makes more money, a lawyer or a doctor? And she said sometimes a lawyer, sometimes a doctor. So he said I'm going to be both.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second one is very interested in everything that it is religious. He said I want to be a rabbi. And the third one, he breaks everything. When he was three years, he took apart all electrical outlets. Everything that's in the house, he breaks. He fixes everything. So he probably will be an engineer. When he comes to me, I have prepared a whole box with old telephones. And he said what do I have to fix today. He sits with a telephone the whole day. And on the end of the day, he said I know what's wrong. The wires are crossed. And this is his--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five years, oh, my God.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says-- and he says the most, he comes by my daughter, all the electrical wore, everything is changed. Only when he want-- for a present, when I ask him you do you want the best, said he wants a tool box. This is him. And the oldest one tells me jokes. He calls me up-- if he has a joke, he calls me up collect. So every joke cost me $5.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think it's well worth it. And we're done, but we're not done because we're going to invite your wife to join us now. And we want to look at your photographs and at your documents. And we're going to do that in just one minute. And now we're happy to welcome you, Amalia, to our interview. We didn't ask you, Norman, to sit in Amalia's because we knew the two interviews would be together. So will you both summarize, please, if you can, if it's possible for you to summarize? You said the one regret you had, Norman, was that you did not go to live in Palestine, because you would have liked to have made more than a monetary contribution to that country. Amalia, do you feel the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I paid my dues. And I feel that, like everybody else, I deserved a peaceful and quiet life. On top of it, my whole family, who's alive-- which is alive-- is in Palestine, so I-- in Israel-- so I felt I want safety, finally. And, naturally, in any other way that I can, I'll contribute. I educated Jewish children to love Israel, I taught them the language. And naturally, financially, I tried to submit my whole family. And this is my contribution. So I don't have regrets.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Norman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, looking back, how do you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Looking back, like I said, I mentioned before that I don't-- America doesn't owe me anything. And America was very good to me. But, still, looking back, that, as a survivor, in this time, when Israel needed me, I wasn't there. And I feel like a traitor that I didn't go when they fought to establish a homeland after 2,000 years. And I wasn't there. So, to me, I let them down, in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now let me ask you another question. You passed as a Pole. You joined the Polish army just before the end of the Second World War, with a third name. You used many artificial names, false names. Did you ever think of staying in Poland as a Pole, not as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: We spoke. With my wife, we had a discussion. Once we are already established, I had a very good high position in the military. My wife was already acknowledged as being Felicia Merashevska, as a Pole.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we asked the question, should we cut off with Judaism? For future generations, they shouldn't go through what my father had to be killed, and goes in, the inquisition, crusaders, and all those things. Maybe it's enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife said she cannot do it because she has all her family in Israel. And I said, I cannot do it for other reason. I told her that I am a Kohen. A Kohen is from the priest family. Not that I believe that this is something special, but we are going back, our lineage is going back till Aaron, the brother of Moses. This is almost 4,000 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during the 4,000 years, the Jews were very strict that a son took over the priestlihood from his father. Something that has lasted for so many years, so thousand years, so many thousand years. How many were killed for this being a Jew, like my father was killed? And maybe his forefathers were killed for being a Jew. Who am I, that after so many thousands of years, that I should be the one and cut the chain?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was my reason that I am not-- I cannot take on myself to say that they all suffered and went through all the inquisitions, all the discriminations, all the killings, and, by me, it should end. And I said this is the main reasons that I cannot remain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter has given you the gift of three grandchildren that you are educating with a Jewish education. And so, hopefully, they will carry on the lineage. I want to thank both of you very much for participating. Norman, you're not finished yet, because now we have to identify all of these wonderful, wonderful photographs and the documents that we have. And we still have a lot more that we have to say. Did you want to add something, Amalia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, please. I would like to add, too. I, first of all-- I wanted to be a Amalia Petrankiya, and not to be Felicia Merashevska. And as Felicia, Amalia Petrankiya, I didn't have a future in Poland. I saw the killings, the pogroms and killed. And I vividly remember, as Amalia Petrankiya, and going to school on holidays, Christian holidays-- our school had to ask for police help, and close the gate-- special spiked iron gate-- because they were stoning us. Coming over in school yard and hitting us. Then I saw, during the war, all the one who adored me, who professed love and wanted to marry me, said to me confidentially one thing Hitler has done good for us, cleaning Poland of Jews. So after the war, I saw again killing the Jews. I knew definitely that my future cannot be in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to leave it, and as soon as possible. Because living under Soviet Union, I knew they will close the gate around us and we will not be able to leave. So therefore, I persuaded my husband. And just a few months, after the liberation, to escape, to leave it, and start a new life where we will be accepted.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must add one thing. I had reunion in '74, when I was in Warsaw. I met the Kasjia, the Polish Christian girl who helped me to obtain the job. And before she knew I'm Jewish, because she didn't know. She was so happy to see, and my daughter. And when I told her, you know, Kasjia, I am Jewish. Suddenly, their whole attitude changed. She did not what to ask for me-- material things, financial things, how to use me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then she said to me, are you coming back to Poland for another visit? This was in '74. I said, Kasjia, I like to come when I am welcomed. I don't like to come where I still have to hide that I'm Jewish. So this is the reason that I'm happy about our decision, and the reasons why I didn't want to stay in Poland. I certainly can relate and understand how you feel. And, again, I thank both of you so much for a truly, truly wonderful contribution to the Shoah Foundation. We look forward to having Norman explain the photographs. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture represents my grandmother and my grandfather, my father's parents. Her name, my grandmother's name was Gela Saleschutz. And my grandfather's was Naftali Saleschutz, after whom I'm named. My father was called Itchin Naftali, and I'm called Naftali Itchis. This was taken in the village Dubas. Dubas was four kilometer from Kolbuszowa. He's-- he-- all his life, he was a manager in the-- he was the head of the village who managed a whole a village for the owner. This picture was taken on the occasion when my oldest brother Abgum, which became in America Albert, came to visit our town in 1934. It was a very, very important occasion, otherwise my father wouldn't go out and have taken-- a picture taken of him. From the bottom line, from the left, the first person, it's me, Naftali Saleschutz. I was, in this time, 14 years old. The second is Shulem Saleschutz. He's the son of my second brother, Leibush Saleschutz, who is also in the picture. The third person is my youngest sister, Rachel Saleschutz. After Rachel is Shaindle Weinstein. She is the daughter of my oldest sister, Gela Weinstein, who sits above her. The second row from left is Abgum, Albert, Saleschutz, which in this time it was Salsitz already in America. He came for a visit. After him is my mother. Her name is Esther Saleschutz. Then, my father, Isak Saleschutz. After Isak is my oldest sister, Gela Weinstein, Gela Saleschutz Weinstein, who married Ruben Weinstein, who is sitting next to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's a tall man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- it looks-- it looks in that picture that he's very short, but he was the tallest. He was about 6' 4'. And they purposely gave him a very low seat to even him out. But I see that the seat was too low.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, a very high hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it very high hat. The top row is Matla Saleschutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, from the right or left?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left. Matla-- Saleschutz, she is my fourth sister. Next to her is Shi David Leischer the husband of my sister, of my second sister, Malka Saleschutz Leischer, who's next to him. After Malka is my brother, David. This picture was inserted after the picture was taken because he was in this time in Palestine. And he wanted to complete all-- we wanted to complete all our nine children and the wives, if they were married, or the husbands. So we put in this picture after it was done. Next to him is Hamcha Saleschutz. She is the wife of my second brother, Leibush Saleschutz, who is next to her, who are the parents of Shulem Saleschutz, the youngest sitting on the floor. Next to Leibush, is Liba Saleschutz. All the three sisters in this time were not married yet. But then after, they were married and all of them had children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us what happened to this entire family, the survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: This entire family was killed in one day, except Albert, who died in America, my father, who was shot in our home in April 1942 by the Gestapo, and David, who is alive and he still lives here in America. He came over here from Palestine after I arrived to America. And Leibush, who survived with me the war in the camps, and also two years in the woods. And he died in 1960 a natural death in America.
